Mark Scheme · Predicted Paper 1 (Advanced) | 4EC1/01


                                Mark Scheme (Predicted)
                                            ADVANCED
                                Pearson Edexcel International GCSE
                                     in Economics (4EC1) Paper 01
                          Paper 1: Microeconomics and Business Economics

General Marking Guidance
• All candidates must receive the same treatment.
• Mark schemes should be applied positively. Candidates must be rewarded for what they have shown they
can do rather than penalised for omissions.
• Examiners should mark according to the mark scheme, not according to their perception of where grade
boundaries may lie.
• All marks on the mark scheme are designed to be awarded. Award full marks if the response matches the
mark scheme; award zero if the response is not worthy of credit.
• Crossed out work should be marked UNLESS the candidate has replaced it with an alternative response.


                                                    Page 1
                                                       Mark Scheme · Predicted Paper 1 (Advanced) | 4EC1/01


Question   Which one of the following values shows unitary price elasticity of         Mark
number     demand?
           Answer
1(a)       AO1 1 mark                                                                       (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           C –1
           A is not correct because this is perfectly price inelastic demand
           B is not correct because this is price inelastic demand
           D is not correct because this is price elastic demand


Question   A worker earns €12.00 per hour for a 35-hour week plus 6 hours of           Mark
number     overtime at 1.75× the hourly rate. Total gross wage.
           Answer
1(b)       AO2 1 mark                                                                       (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           C €546.00
           Working: (35 × €12.00) + (6 × €12.00 × 1.75) = €420.00 + €126.00 =
           €546.00
           A is not correct because this is only the standard 35 hours
           B is not correct because this assumes 41 hours at the standard rate
           D is not correct because this is 41 hours paid at 1.75×, which double-
           counts the standard hours


Question   What is meant by the term planned economy?                                  Mark
number     Answer
1(c)       AO1 2 marks                                                                      (2)
           Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition
           An economy in which the government/state (1) makes the decisions
           about the allocation of resources / what, how and for whom to
           produce (1)
           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   State one example of an occupation in the tertiary sector.                  Mark
number     Answer
1(d)       AO1 1 mark                                                                       (1)
           • Teacher / Retailer / Banker / Doctor / Hairdresser / Nurse
           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   Define the term factor of production.                                       Mark
number     Answer


                                              Page 2
                                                       Mark Scheme · Predicted Paper 1 (Advanced) | 4EC1/01

1(e)       AO1 1 mark                                                                       (1)
           A resource used to produce goods and services · land, labour, capital
           or enterprise
           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   Calculate PED: price +15%, quantity demanded −24%.                          Mark
number     Answer
1(f)       AO2 2 marks                                                                      (2)
           Award 1 mark for showing correct calculation
           –24% ÷ 15% (1)
           Award 1 mark for correct PED
           = –1.60 (1)
           Award 2 marks if PED is correctly calculated as –1.6 or –1.60, even if
           no calculations are shown
           Do not award marks for the formula alone


Question   Effect on the solar panel market of a producer subsidy.                     Mark
number     Answer
1(g)                                                                                        (3)


           AO2 3 marks
           Award 1 mark for a rightward shift of the supply curve, labelled (e.g.
           S1)
           Award 1 mark for a lower equilibrium price, labelled (e.g. P1)
           Award 1 mark for a higher equilibrium quantity, labelled (e.g. Q1)


Question   Explain one possible opportunity cost for MediCorp of choosing to           Mark
number     invest in R&D rather than paying dividends.
           Answer
1(h)       AO2 3 marks                                                                      (3)

                                              Page 3
                                                           Mark Scheme · Predicted Paper 1 (Advanced) | 4EC1/01


               Award 1 mark for identifying a relevant opportunity cost
               Award 1 mark for developing the opportunity cost
               Award 1 mark for the response being in context
               • One opportunity cost is shareholders not receiving dividend income
               (1). Shareholders will not receive a cash payment from the $420
               million profit (1), which is the next best alternative use of the funds,
               sacrificed by investing in R&D (1)
               Accept any other appropriate response (e.g. share price may fall in the
               short term, loss of shareholder confidence)


Question       Analyse two possible factors influencing Sony's decision to relocate its camera
number         assembly to Vietnam.
               Indicative content
1(i)                                       AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)
                • Factors influencing business location decisions include production costs
                  (especially labour), proximity to markets, availability of skilled labour, government
                  policy and exchange rates.
                • Labour costs in Vietnam are significantly lower than in Japan, which would reduce
                  Sony's variable costs and lower average cost per camera.
                • The growing South-East Asian consumer market means producing close to
                  customers reduces transport costs and delivery times, improving responsiveness.
                • However, Sony faces set-up costs, worker training and quality-control risks, which
                  may offset some of the savings · the firm must weigh these against the long-
                  term cost reductions.
                • Sony's decision will also depend on whether Vietnamese workers can quickly
                  reach the productivity levels of Japanese workers; if not, quality could suffer and
                  consumer confidence in the Sony brand may fall.
                • Exchange rate and political stability in Vietnam may also influence the decision,
                  since fluctuations can affect overall cost savings.

                           Interpretation of economic information will be limited, with a lack of
                           analysis of issues (AO3).

Level 2    3–4           • Demonstrates partial knowledge and understanding by developing
                           relevant points. Partial application of economic terms, concepts, theories
                           and calculations (AO2).
                         • Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                           organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be good, with

                    and calculations (AO2).
                  • Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity and
                    organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be excellent,
                    with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).

Question   Which diagram shows the effect of an increase in the cost of raw             Mark
number     materials?
           Answer
2(a)       AO2 1 mark                                                                        (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           B Leftward shift of the supply curve, higher price, lower quantity
           A is not correct because this shows a fall in costs / subsidy
           C is not correct because this shows a rise in demand
           D is not correct because this shows a fall in demand


Question   Which one of the following is a feature of monopolistic competition?         Mark
number     Answer
2(b)       AO1 1 mark                                                                        (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           C Firms sell differentiated products and have some price-making
           ability
           A is not correct because this describes a monopoly
           B is not correct because this describes perfect competition
           D is not correct because barriers to entry are low in monopolistic
           competition


Question   Calculate the percentage change in the firm's total revenue when             Mark
number     price rises from $40 to $44 and quantity falls from 1,000 to 900.
           Answer
2(c)       AO2 2 marks                                                                       (2)
           Award 1 mark for showing correct calculation
           Old TR = $40 × 1,000 = $40,000
           New TR = $44 × 900 = $39,600 (1)
           Award 1 mark for correct percentage change
           % change = (($39,600 − $40,000) ÷ $40,000) × 100 = −1.00% (1)
           Award 2 marks if the percentage change is correctly calculated as −1%
           or −1.00%, even if no calculations are shown
           Do not award marks for the formula alone


Question   What is meant by the term merit good?                                        Mark
number     Answer
2(d)       AO1 2 marks                                                                       (2)
           Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition
           A good that is underconsumed / under-provided in a free market (1)
           because consumers undervalue the private benefits and/or the good


                                               Page 6
                                                           Mark Scheme · Predicted Paper 1 (Advanced) | 4EC1/01

           generates positive externalities (1)
           Accept any other appropriate response (e.g. examples such as
           education or healthcare may be used to support the definition)


Question   Describe one feature of a monopoly.                                             Mark
number     Answer
2(e)       AO1 2 marks                                                                          (2)
           Award 1 mark for reference to the feature and 1 mark for
           development
           Single firm dominates the market (1) so it has significant market
           power / is a price-maker (1)
           OR
           High barriers to entry (1) so few or no competitors can enter the
           market (1)
           OR
           Abnormal profits in the long run (1) because there is no competitive
           pressure to reduce price (1)
           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   Explain one reason why Piotr may want to donate to the food bank                Mark
number     rather than maximise the profits received by the owner.
           Answer
2(f)       AO2 3 marks                                                                          (3)
           Award 1 mark for identifying a relevant reason
           Award 1 mark for developing the reason
           Award 1 mark for the response being in context
           • Managers may pursue social responsibility (1). Donating to a local
           food bank improves the firm's reputation and brand image (1) which,
           although it reduces Olga's profits by 10%, may attract more ethical
           consumers to the organic food store (1)
           • Managers may have different objectives to owners (1). Piotr may
           personally value helping the community (1) and donating instead of
           distributing to Olga, the owner, achieves his non-profit goal (1)
           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   Assess the effectiveness of tradable pollution permits when dealing with negative
number     externalities such as carbon emissions.
           Indicative content
2(g)                           AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3 marks)
            • Negative externalities are costs imposed on third parties as a result of
              production/consumption · carbon emissions are an example because they
              contribute to climate change affecting society at large.
            • Tradable permits place a cap on total emissions and allow firms to buy or sell


                                                  Page 7
                                                         Mark Scheme · Predicted Paper 1 (Advanced) | 4EC1/01

                 permits, meaning the market decides how emission cuts are made.
              • Firms that can cut emissions cheaply have an incentive to do so and sell their
                spare permits, so reductions are made at the lowest cost · this is economically
                efficient.
              • The cap guarantees an overall fall in emissions, which directly addresses the
                externality.
              • The EU ETS has raised the cost of emitting CO₂ for polluting industries,
                incentivising investment in cleaner technology.
              • Therefore, tradable permits can be an effective market-based solution to
                environmental externalities.
              • AO4
              • However, permit prices in the EU ETS have been volatile, making it hard for firms
                to plan long-term investment in low-carbon technology.
              • Free allocation of permits to some industries (e.g. heavy manufacturing) weakens
                the price signal and undermines the policy.
              • Monitoring and enforcing the cap requires administrative resources · there is an
                opportunity cost and the risk of fraud.
              • Regulation (e.g. strict emission limits) gives more certainty on outcomes and may
                be easier to enforce, even if less cost-efficient.
              • The overall effectiveness depends on the level of the cap, whether it is tightened
                over time, and the coverage of industries. A combination of permits and
                regulation may be more effective than either alone.

Question   Which is most likely to cause a decrease in demand for labour?               Mark
number     Answer
3(a)       AO1 1 mark                                                                        (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           C The introduction of labour-saving automation
           A is not correct because higher demand for the product increases
           derived demand for labour
           B is not correct because higher productivity typically increases
           demand for labour
           D is not correct because lower training costs make labour cheaper
           relative to capital


Question   Which formula calculates total revenue?                                      Mark
number     Answer
3(b)       AO1 1 mark                                                                        (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           A Price × quantity sold
           B is not correct because this gives total revenue only as an identity,
           not the calculation
           C is not correct because dividing does not calculate revenue
           D is not correct because adding does not calculate revenue

number     Answer
3(c)                                                                                         (3)


           AO2 3 marks

               Award 1 mark for showing no change in quantity on the quantity axis


Question       Analyse the possible advantages for Rolex of using specialisation in its production
number         process.
               Indicative content
3(d)                                       AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)
                • Specialisation (division of labour) involves breaking the production process into
                  smaller tasks, with each worker focused on one specific task.
                • At Rolex, individual craftsmen specialise in elements such as movement assembly,
                  engraving or dial fitting · they develop very high skill in their narrow task.
                • This high skill level translates into higher quality output, which is essential for
                  Rolex's reputation as a premium brand.
                • Specialisation also raises productivity because skilled workers complete their
                  tasks more quickly and accurately, lowering the time needed per watch.
                • Over time, training costs are lower per worker because each person only needs to
                  learn one stage, and experienced workers can train newcomers.
                • The very high quality and brand reputation that results from specialisation justify
                  Rolex's premium prices, allowing it to earn abnormal profits in the long run.

Question       Assess whether demand is likely to be more price elastic for designer branded
number         clothing than for bread.
               Indicative content
3(e)                              AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3 marks)
                • Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the responsiveness of quantity
                  demanded to a change in price.
                • Designer clothing, such as Gucci or Balenciaga, is a luxury and takes up a large
                  share of consumer income, making demand responsive to price changes ·
                  therefore more price elastic.
                • Designer clothing has close substitutes: consumers can switch to cheaper branded
                  or non-branded clothing if the price of Gucci rises, reinforcing elasticity.
                • Bread, by contrast, is a necessity and a staple food. Consumers must continue
                  buying it to meet basic dietary needs, making demand price inelastic.
                • Bread is inexpensive · a small percentage rise in price means only a small
                  absolute increase in spending, so quantity demanded changes little.
                • Demand for designer branded clothing is therefore likely to be more price elastic
                  than demand for bread.
                • AO4
                • However, demand for designer brands may be price inelastic among wealthy
                  consumers, for whom the price is a small share of income and the status of the
                  brand matters more than cost.
                • Demand for bread may be more price elastic than expected because there are
                  many close substitutes such as rice, pasta and breakfast cereals.
                • Own-brand supermarket bread may have price elastic demand even though bread
                  overall is a necessity, because customers can easily switch between supermarket
                  brands.
                • In the long run, fashions change · consumers may become less attached to
                  specific designer labels, raising elasticity over time.
                • On balance, designer branded clothing is typically more price elastic than bread,
                  but within each market the elasticity depends on the brand, the consumer group
                  and the time horizon.

Question       Calculate the daily total revenue earned by the airline.                     Mark
number         Answer
4(a)           AO2 2 marks                                                                       (2)
               Award 1 mark for showing correct calculation
               Total passengers per day = 24 × 150 = 3,600
               Total revenue = 3,600 × $85 (1)
               Award 1 mark for calculating correct total revenue
               = $306,000 (1)
               Award 2 marks if total revenue is correctly calculated as $306,000,
               even if no calculations are shown
               Do not award marks for the formula alone


Question       Analyse how internal economies of scale may lead to benefits for Samsung.
number         Indicative content
4(b)                                       AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)
                • Internal economies of scale occur when average costs fall as a firm expands its
                  output · examples include purchasing, marketing, technical, managerial,
                  financial and risk-bearing economies.
                • Samsung's global scale means it buys components (e.g. glass, semiconductors) in
                  very large quantities, earning substantial bulk discounts · purchasing economies.
                • The firm invests $30 billion per year in R&D, which is spread over millions of units
                  sold worldwide, lowering the average R&D cost per device.
                • The highly automated Pyeongtaek semiconductor plant is a technical economy ·
                  such advanced plants only become cost-effective at high volumes.
                • Marketing costs for global campaigns are spread over a vast output, lowering the
                  average marketing cost per unit.
                • Risk-bearing economies arise because Samsung's product portfolio spans
                  smartphones, TVs, chips and appliances · weakness in one segment can be offset
                  by another.
                • All these economies reduce Samsung's average costs, allowing lower prices,
                  higher margins or greater investment in future products.

Question       Evaluate whether consumers in Kenya are likely to have benefited from the
number         deregulation of the telecommunications industry.
               Indicative content
4(c)                              AO2 (4 marks) / AO3 (4 marks) / AO4 (4 marks)
                • Deregulation is the removal of government restrictions on entry, pricing or
                  operation in a market, which allows more private-sector competition.
                • Kenya's telecoms market was previously a state-owned monopoly with high
                  prices and low coverage.
                • Allowing private firms like Safaricom and Airtel to compete has forced providers
                  to cut prices to win customers, directly benefiting consumers.
                • Mobile phone ownership has risen from a very low base to over 95% of adults,
                  giving consumers access to services they could not previously afford.
                • Competition has driven innovation · M-PESA transformed financial access,
                  allowing millions of previously unbanked Kenyans to send money, receive wages
                  and save digitally.
                • Greater product choice (more networks, more tariffs, new services) gives
                  consumers more flexibility to find plans that suit their needs.
                • Therefore, consumers appear to have benefited significantly from deregulation.
                • AO4
                • However, critics argue that market power has concentrated in a small number of
                  firms · Safaricom in particular has a dominant share, creating oligopolistic
                  conditions where prices are no longer pushed as aggressively.
                • Rural users still face limited coverage and higher prices because providers focus
                  investment on more profitable urban areas · so benefits are uneven.
                • Without effective regulation, a dominant firm can exploit its position, reducing
                  consumer welfare compared with a competitive market.
                • Deregulation can also reduce quality standards if firms compete only on price and
                  cut investment in network reliability.
                • Some former state employees may have lost jobs during the restructuring of the
                  industry · an important distributional cost, though outside the direct scope of
                  consumer welfare.
                • The overall outcome depends on the strength of the Kenyan communications
                  regulator and whether it prevents dominant firms abusing their position. On
                  balance, most consumers in Kenya have clearly benefited · prices are lower and
                  services are broader · but the gains are less evenly spread in rural areas, and
                  continued regulatory vigilance is needed to preserve competition.

                    organisation. Chain of reasoning will be coherent and logical.
